channel.title: NYT > Business
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Stocks Slide as Investors See Rates Rising After Strong Jobs Data
item.description: The S&P 500 fell more than 2.6 percent on Friday, its worst one-day drop of the year and ending nine weeks of gains.
item.dc:creator: Joe Rennison

item.title: ’60 Minutes’ Stars Will Stay After Pelley’s Firing Because They Don’t Want Show to ‘Die’
item.description: Lesley Stahl, Bill Whitaker and Jon Wertheim said on Friday in an email to the show’s staff that they had reached the decision after a period of “grieving” and frustration.
item.dc:creator: Benjamin Mullin and Michael M. Grynbaum

item.title: What to know about the report.
Central Ohio Becomes Hub for Tech and Manufacturing
item.description: Tech titans and Silicon Valley transplants changed the Columbus area, but not everyone is thrilled about the rapid transformation.
item.dc:creator: Farah Stockman
item.media:credit: Maddie McGarvey for The New York Times

item.title: Elon Musk Dominates List of Highest Paid C.E.O.s
item.description: Executive compensation is accelerating while rank-and-file workers lag, widening the pay gap in corporate America.
item.dc:creator: Jeff Sommer
item.category: Dodd-Frank Wall Street Reform and Consumer Protection Act (2010)
item.media:credit: Photo illustration by Nicolas Ortega; photos by Getty Images

item.title: White House rejoices over strong jobs report.
item.description: The stronger-than-expected report offers President Trump a talking point for the midterms, even as it also reduces the odds that the Federal Reserve may cut rates.
item.dc:creator: Tony Romm

item.title: More Renters Are Using Tools to Skip Security Deposits, but There’s a Catch
item.description: Third-party services charge fees to help renters bypass some upfront costs of signing a lease. Unlike traditional deposits, the money won’t be returned to tenants.
item.dc:creator: Ann Carrns

item.title: Anthropic’s Call for A.I. Nonproliferation
item.description: The artificial intelligence giant said a “brake pedal” was needed to protect humanity from self-improving models. The proposal could have big consequences.
item.dc:creator: Andrew Ross Sorkin, Bernhard Warner, Sarah Kessler, Michael J. de la Merced, Niko Gallogly, Brian O’Keefe and Ian Mount
item.media:credit: Jason Henry for The New York Times
item.media:description: Does the world need to consider a kind of A.I. nonproliferation treaty?

item.title: With Jobs Market Stable, Fed Is Focusing on Inflation Over Rate Cuts
item.description: Officials at the Federal Reserve have turned their focus to resurgent inflation.
item.dc:creator: Colby Smith

item.title: U.S. Job Market Pushes Past Shocks and Strains
item.description: Employers added 172,000 jobs in May, adding to a vigorous pace of hiring in recent months. But wage growth is not keeping up with higher prices and consumers remain pessimistic.
item.dc:creator: Lydia DePillis

item.title: How the Job Market Is Leaving New Graduates Behind
item.description: Sydney Ember, a Times business reporter, has been speaking with recent college graduates struggling to find work. She explains why starting a career in the current economy could leave lasting scars on wages and opportunities.
item.dc:creator: Sydney Ember, Nour Idriss and Stephanie Swart

item.title: To Sell Trucks, Break Out the Cowboys and Wrap Them in Old Glory
item.description: Americans — especially American men — love trucks, and high gas prices aren’t swaying automakers. Ram has a new line of fuel-guzzling muscle trucks, and some in-your-face ads to sell them.
item.dc:creator: Roy Furchgott
item.media:description: The 2027 Ram 1500 Rumble Bee SRT. “Ram buyers like other people to know that they are driving a Ram truck,” one analyst said.

item.title: Crowded Airport Lounges Are Rolling Out Grab-and-Go Options
item.description: New premium pit stops are giving fliers a place for a quick bite and drink without having to line up for increasingly crowded traditional lounges.
item.dc:creator: Julie Weed

item.title: Can He-Man’s ‘Masters of the Universe’ Generate the Same Movie Magic as Barbie?
item.description: “Masters of the Universe” will test whether Mattel can cement itself as an entertainment powerhouse with a hypermasculine character from the 1980s.
item.dc:creator: Nicole Sperling

item.title: A Job Market Leaving Young Graduates Behind Could Scar Them for Years
item.description: The labor market has improved, but people entering the work force are having a harder time starting careers, a dynamic that has had permanent effects in the past.
item.dc:creator: Sydney Ember

item.title: When Jeffrey Epstein Needed Favors, This Restaurant Mogul Was There
item.description: Stephen Hanson, whose empire included Blue Water Grill and Ruby Foo’s, was a devoted friend and wingman who helped manage and entertain the women in Epstein’s orbit.
item.dc:creator: Kim Severson

item.title: China Builds an Economic Fortress as Global Tensions Rise
item.description: Beijing says the changes are needed for national security, but they could complicate efforts by Chinese companies to find growth overseas.
item.dc:creator: Alexandra Stevenson and Murphy Zhao
item.media:description: A robot made by a Chinese company displayed in April at an expo in Guangzhou, China. Beijing unveiled rules this week that would slow the overseas expansion of Chinese companies.

item.title: Nick Bilton, New ‘60 Minutes’ Chief, Pledges Independence
item.description: Nick Bilton said he had consulted with the program’s remaining correspondents: Lesley Stahl, Jon Wertheim and Bill Whitaker. All three were deliberating whether to stay with the show, two people said.
item.dc:creator: Benjamin Mullin and Michael M. Grynbaum
item.media:description: Nick Bilton, the executive producer of “60 Minutes.”

item.title: Nose Gear on Boeing 787-9 Dreamliner Collapses, Injuring Several Workers
item.description: The airline Lufthansa said the cause of the accident at Frankfurt Airport was under investigation. The plane can weigh 279 tons at takeoff.
item.dc:creator: Michael Levenson
item.media:description: Crew members who were preparing the plane ahead of a flight to Los Angeles were injured.

item.title: Wall Street Is Going Gaga for SpaceX
item.description: Jamie Dimon himself is planning to pitch investors on the offering of Elon Musk’s rocket company, as banks prepare to reap huge fees from the largest I.P.O. ever.
item.dc:creator: Lauren Hirsch
item.media:description: SpaceX signage in the lobby of the Goldman Sachs headquarters in New York.

item.title: Supreme Court Finds S.E.C. Can Strip Wrongdoers of Illegal Financial Gains, Even Without Proof of Victim Loss
item.description: The justices unanimously sided with the independent financial watchdog, finding that the agency could collect ill-gotten money, even if it could not show victims had suffered financial loss.
item.dc:creator: Abbie VanSickle
item.media:credit: Alyssa Schukar for The New York Times
item.media:description: In a unanimous decision written by Justice Neil M. Gorsuch, the justices sided with the Securities and Exchange Commission.

item.title: ‘60 Minutes’ Turmoil: What to Know After Scott Pelley’s Firing
item.description: Scott Pelley’s firing is the latest in a string of controversies and staffing shake-ups that have plagued CBS’s news division.
item.dc:creator: Sophia June
item.media:credit: Lucia Vazquez for The New York Times
item.media:description: The CBS News studios in Midtown Manhattan.

item.title: Netflix’s New Movie Strategy: Fewer, Better Films
item.description: Dan Lin doesn’t fawn over stars or write blank checks. And he still greenlights more movies than anyone.
item.dc:creator: Nicole Sperling
item.media:credit: Ahmed Gaber for The New York Times
item.media:description: Dan Lin, the chairman of Netflix’s film division.

item.title: How LinkedIn Found Its Social Platform Era
item.description: Celebrities and paid influencers have transformed the career-focused platform, which for some users, has become a job unto itself.
item.dc:creator: Isabella Kwai
item.media:credit: Annie Flanagan for The New York Times
item.media:description: “This whole corporate, ‘move the needle,’ synergistic stuff — I think people are tired of it,” said Brooke Sweedar, a content creator on LinkedIn. “You have to do something else.”

item.title: Gwynne Shotwell, Elon Musk’s No. 2 at SpaceX, Is the Company’s Steady Hand
item.description: Gwynne Shotwell, the president and chief operating officer, is the adult-in-the-room foil to Mr. Musk as SpaceX prepares for a blockbuster initial public offering.
item.dc:creator: Ryan Mac
item.media:description: Gwynne Shotwell, SpaceX’s president and chief operating officer, in Miami Beach last year during one of her infrequent public appearances. “I love working for Elon,” she has said.

item.title: American Farms Rely More Than Ever on the Troubled H-2A Visa Program
item.description: The Trump administration is allowing in more agricultural guest workers under the H-2A program, but preventing abuses is proving difficult.
item.dc:creator: Lydia DePillis
item.media:credit: Grant Hindsley for The New York Times
item.media:description: Christian Calderon is among the H-2A visa workers hired to harvest cherries in Kennewick, Wash. As the Trump administration lowers wages, farmers have requested more foreign workers.

item.title: Inside SpaceX’s Rockets-to-Riches I.P.O. Plan
item.description: The latest prospectus by Elon Musk’s company underscores its confidence in its planned market debut. But skeptics see reasons for concern.
item.dc:creator: Andrew Ross Sorkin, Bernhard Warner, Sarah Kessler, Michael J. de la Merced, Niko Gallogly, Brian O’Keefe and Lauren Hirsch
item.media:description: SpaceX appears confident that its expected I.P.O. will soar.

item.title: The U.S.-Qatar Domination of Gas Left the World Dangerously Exposed
item.description: Before the war, the global market for liquefied natural gas was increasingly commanded by just two countries, one of which has now been hobbled.
item.dc:creator: River Akira Davis
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: The liquefied natural gas production facility in Qatar’s Ras Laffan Industrial City before the war. Iranian attacks have crippled its production, and the damage will take years to repair.

item.title: The Small-Business Owners Managing Whole Armies of A.I. Employees
item.description: When you turn A.I. agents loose on your finances, email and customers, what could possibly go wrong?
item.dc:creator: Clive Thompson

item.title: Can These ChatGPT Ads Make You Love A.I.?
item.description: A campaign for ChatGPT relies on heartwarming retro vibes to sell a product that has become a source of concern for most Americans.
item.dc:creator: Steven Kurutz
item.media:description: Billboards for ChatGPT, part of a new ad campaign, rise above a corner tavern in the Greenwich Village neighborhood of Manhattan.

item.title: Pace Gallery Cuts 50 Artists and 50 Staff Amid Art Market Challenges
item.description: A challenging art market is forcing even an established player to contract.
item.dc:creator: Robin Pogrebin
item.media:credit: Clement Pascal for The New York Times
item.media:description: “The whole art gallery art system became too big, too commercial, too impersonal and too corporate,” Marc Glimcher, the chief executive of Pace Gallery, said.

item.title: Flesh-Eating Pest Confirmed in U.S. Cattle
item.description: The New World screwworm was found in a calf in South Texas, the Agriculture Department said, the first case in the country since the 1960s.
item.dc:creator: Kevin Draper
item.media:description: The New World screwworm fly has been moving north since it was detected at the Guatemalan-Mexican border in 2024.

item.title: SpaceX IPO to Be Largest Ever at $135 Share Price
item.description: The $135 share price means Elon Musk’s rocket maker is poised to exceed the 2019 initial public offering of Saudi Aramco in both valuation and money raised.
item.dc:creator: Ryan Mac
item.media:description: SpaceX’s Starship rocket lifted off last month during a test flight from Starbase in Texas.

item.title: Trump Administration Turns to a New Rationale to Justify Old Tariffs
item.description: The administration has settled on a more legally and politically durable way to impose tariffs, but some say the focus on forced labor laws is merely a pretext for protectionism.
item.dc:creator: Ana Swanson
item.media:description: On Tuesday, the Trump administration announced a tariff of 10 percent to 12.5 percent on 59 countries and the 27-member European Union.

item.title: Trump Administration Fights Court Order to Refund Some Tariffs
item.description: The administration has started to repay some of the money, but has signaled it may make it harder for certain businesses to claim the full amount they are owed.
item.dc:creator: Tony Romm and Ana Swanson
item.media:credit: Alyssa Schukar for The New York Times
item.media:description: The government has indicated that it could hold up the return of potentially tens of billions in previously paid duties.

item.title: Bari Weiss Speaks on Scott Pelley’s ’60 Minutes’ Firing: ‘That’s the Path That He Chose’
item.description: In her first public comments on the firing, Ms. Weiss, the CBS News editor in chief, said that the longtime correspondent had “broken” the trust in the newsroom.
item.dc:creator: Michael M. Grynbaum and Benjamin Mullin
item.media:description: “Despite our attempts to engage with Scott Pelley and to find a way back, unfortunately we weren’t able to do so, and so we had to part ways,” Bari Weiss, the CBS News editor in chief, said on Wednesday.

item.title: European Union Outlines Plan to Reduce Dependence on American Tech
item.description: The 27-nation European Union outlined how it hopes to expand the region’s data centers, semiconductors and cloud computing capabilities.
item.dc:creator: Adam Satariano
item.media:description: European Union flags flutter outside the European Commission headquarters in Brussels.

item.title: What’s Driving Trump’s Big A.I. Pivot
item.description: Even the industry-friendly Trump White House is finding that it needs to have greater oversight of powerful new artificial intelligence models.
item.dc:creator: Andrew Ross Sorkin, Bernhard Warner, Sarah Kessler, Michael J. de la Merced, Niko Gallogly, Brian O’Keefe and Ian Mount
item.media:description: President Trump last year with David Sacks, the administration’s former A.I. czar. Trump’s new executive order on artificial intelligence is a big shift in approach.

item.title: Trump Aims New Tariffs at 59 Countries and the European Union
item.description: Trump officials said they planned to impose levies of up to 12.5 percent on countries that failed to crack down on goods made with forced labor.
item.dc:creator: Catie Edmondson
item.media:description: Cotton being loaded in Xinjiang, China, in 2021.

item.title: Oil Prices Rise as Iran and U.S. Exchange Strikes
item.description: Iranian drones struck Kuwait’s main international airport and the United States and Iran exchanged strikes.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: New Intelligence Role Puts Bill Pulte’s Housing Agenda in Doubt
item.description: Mr. Pulte has had difficulty boosting the housing market as head of the Federal Housing Finance Agency. Now he will also serve as acting intelligence director.
item.dc:creator: Matthew Goldstein and Lydia DePillis
item.category: Office of the Director of National Intelligence
Federal Home Loan Mortgage Corp (Freddie Mac)
item.media:description: Bill Pulte, the director of the Federal Housing Finance Agency, had some early success in bringing down mortgage rates, but housing prices have remained high.

item.title: For Knicks’ Owner James Dolan, a Title Might Finally Stop Some Boos
item.description: James Dolan, the longtime owner of the N.B.A. team, has faced years of jeers. Could a championship change all that?
item.dc:creator: Tania Ganguli and Dionne Searcey
item.media:description: James Dolan, center, at a New York Knicks game in 2023. In his nearly 30 years owning the team, it had one of the worst records in the N.B.A.

item.title: Even if Oil Prices Peak Soon, the Global Economy Will Slow This Year
item.description: The Organization for Economic Cooperation and Development, in a report, said the Middle East war’s consequences “are likely to be felt for some time.”
item.dc:creator: Eshe Nelson
item.category: Organization for Economic Cooperation and Development
US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: An oil refinery in England. Higher energy prices because of the war in Iran will slow global economic growth and raise inflation through 2027, the O.E.C.D. said.

item.title: CBS News Fires Scott Pelley of ‘60 Minutes’
item.description: Mr. Pelley, a former “CBS Evening News” anchor, was ousted after months of tensions between staff and Bari Weiss, the network’s editor in chief.
item.dc:creator: Benjamin Mullin and Michael M. Grynbaum
item.media:description: Scott Pelley was fired a day after a tense meeting where he accused the CBS editor in chief, Bari Weiss, of “murdering ‘60 Minutes.’”

item.title: NOTUS Cannot Be ‘The Star’: Judge Blocks D.C. News Outlet’s Rebranding
item.description: NOTUS, a political news website, must temporarily suspend its plans to rebrand, a judge ruled in a trademark dispute brought by The Washington Star.
item.dc:creator: Katie Robertson
item.media:description: The original Washington Star, which was once called The Washington Star-News, was a conservative-leaning newspaper.

item.title: Colorado Enacts Law Tightening Scrutiny of Uber and Lyft
item.description: The law cites a New York Times investigation that found Uber received reports of sexual assault or sexual misconduct far more often than the company had publicly disclosed.
item.dc:creator: Emily Steel
item.media:description: Jenny Willford, the Colorado state representative who introduced the legislation.

item.title: White House Correspondents Dinner Rescheduled for July
item.description: The dinner was abruptly cut short in April when a gunman disrupted the proceedings. President Trump said that he would attend.
item.dc:creator: Michael M. Grynbaum
item.media:credit: Salwan Georges for The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump and the first lady, Melania Trump, at the White House Correspondents’ Association dinner in April.

item.title: MSC Ship Is Struck by Two Projectiles in Iraqi Port
item.description: The attack on the vessel, owned by the Mediterranean Shipping Company, underlines the threat to ships even as the U.S. and Iran try to negotiate reopening the Strait of Hormuz.
item.dc:creator: Jenny Gross
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Vessels in the Strait of Hormuz. As part of the U.S. blockade against Iran, the United States has disabled five commercial vessels and redirected 121 as of Monday.
